title: Non-blanching rash and shock in a 17-year-old
presentation: A 17-year-old male is brought in by ambulance with 6 hours of fever, severe headache, and a rapidly spreading rash. His parents say the rash started as a few spots and has spread dramatically over 2 hours. Vitals: BP 72/40, HR 148, RR 28, Temp 40.2C, SpO2 94%. Exam: widespread non-blanching purpuric rash covering trunk, limbs and face, meningismus, GCS 12, cold mottled peripheries.

immediate: blood cultures immediately then IV ceftriaxone WITHOUT delay
urgent: LP only if stable after resuscitation
gold_standard_workup.key_decision_point: Non-blanching purpuric rash + septic shock = meningococcemia. Give IV ceftriaxone IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for LP, cultures, or anything else. Every minute of delay increases mortality. DIC is a major complication — monitor coags closely. ICU mandatory.
